user_prompt: Please implement the code as described by the comment on lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:3
Implement a class to represent a FIFO used for audio data
The data stored shall be 16 bit mono PCM always. The buffers are likely to be around 4096 samples large but make it configurable via constructor
If the data arriving is 8 KHz, it will be upsampled to 16 KHz before being queued into to the FIFO.
If the output needs to be stereo, it will be transformed after being dequeued from the FIFO

There needs to be a state-machine. I want the consumer to not start consuming until the FIFO is filled to a certain amount (configurable via constructor), after that, it may consume until it is empty.
The latency (in milliseconds) between when the FIFO was empty to when the FIFO has allowed dequeuing must be recorded.
The queue and dequeue functions must handle blocks, not just sample-wise or byte-wise
The FIFOs going to the file require no watermark, but the other FIFOs must use the watermark feature to prevent glitches
Replace the comment with your implementation.
Optimize for speed. Don't over-explore or over-check your work other than what is absolutely necessary to implement the comment.
Once you are done, remove the comment.
agent_reply: - 8 kHz input upsampled to 16 kHz on queue
- Watermark state machine: fill first, drain until empty

user_prompt: thanks, I made it more generic, check over it
agent_reply: - [lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:165](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:165): `queueUpsampled8kLocked()` returns `inputToWrite * 2`, while `queue16kLocked()` returns source samples consumed. That makes `queue()`’s return value inconsistent. A caller feeding 8 kHz data may interpret the return as input samples consumed and advance too far. I’d return `inputToWrite` from the 8 kHz path, or rename/document the API as returning internal 16 kHz samples queued.

- [lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:37](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:37): any non-8 kHz input is silently treated as 16 kHz. If this class is now more generic, that can hide bad call sites. Consider accepting only `8000` and `16000`, returning `0` or an error/status for unsupported rates.

- [lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:122](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:122): `readyToDequeue()` does not call `updateWatermarkLocked()`. Today that is okay because `queue()` updates the state, but it makes readiness depend on writers always going through this exact path. Tiny robustness improvement: call `updateWatermarkLocked()` here too, but that requires making it non-`const` or marking state fields mutable.

Everything else looks structurally sound: ring buffer wrap logic is good, mono-to-stereo dequeue handles wrap correctly, empty-to-watermark latency is captured, and the state machine does the intended “fill, then drain until empty” behavior.

- [lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:165](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:165): `queueUpsampled8kLocked()` returns `inputToWrite * 2`, while `queue16kLocked()` returns source samples consumed. That makes `queue()`’s return value inconsistent. A caller feeding 8 kHz data may interpret the return as input samples consumed and advance too far. I’d return `inputToWrite` from the 8 kHz path, or rename/document the API as returning internal 16 kHz samples queued.

- [lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:37](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:37): any non-8 kHz input is silently treated as 16 kHz. If this class is now more generic, that can hide bad call sites. Consider accepting only `8000` and `16000`, returning `0` or an error/status for unsupported rates.

- [lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:122](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:122): `readyToDequeue()` does not call `updateWatermarkLocked()`. Today that is okay because `queue()` updates the state, but it makes readiness depend on writers always going through this exact path. Tiny robustness improvement: call `updateWatermarkLocked()` here too, but that requires making it non-`const` or marking state fields mutable.

Everything else looks structurally sound: ring buffer wrap logic is good, mono-to-stereo dequeue handles wrap correctly, empty-to-watermark latency is captured, and the state machine does the intended “fill, then drain until empty” behavior.

user_prompt: inside `lib\AudioFifo\AudioFifo.h`, why does both `getFillPercentagle` and `getFillPercentage` exist?
agent_reply: Because `getFillPercentagle()` is almost certainly a typo that became part of the API, and `getFillPercentage()` was later added as the correctly spelled wrapper.
In [AudioFifo.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h), the typo version does the real work:
and the correctly spelled one just forwards to it:

So functionally they are identical. The likely reason both exist is backward compatibility: some code may have used the misspelled name, then the correctly spelled name was added without breaking existing callers.
If you want to clean it up, the tidy path is: search for `getFillPercentagle`, replace any callers with `getFillPercentage`, then remove the misspelled method.

agent_reply: - [AudioFifo.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:282): the “full” test is slightly too loose: `usedSamples_ >= (capacitySamples_ - 1)` can report `100` when the FIFO is one sample short of full. In normal current capacities, the `percentage >= 100` branch will only be reached when `usedSamples_ == capacitySamples_`, so it probably won’t bite today. But it contradicts the comment and makes the edge behavior fragile. I’d make the condition exact:

The main idea looks good: for the recorder’s `kPumpTargetFillPercentage = 0`, returning `1` for any non-empty FIFO means the pump loop won’t falsely stop while a tiny unread tail remains. That matches your intent nicely.
No major correctness issues beyond the off-by-one-ish full check and readability of the nested ternary.

user_prompt: We have made a lot of progress, a lot of things are working the way I want
Walk through the documentation I wrote earlier, we might've deviated a lot. Warn me where the implementation does not match the written documentation, and tell me suggested changes
Note: I am thinking about making the device way more secure, and the `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` will eventually go away, so ignore things that involve that
agent_reply: 1. **The documented file-transfer/filesystem design no longer matches the code.**

The docs still say Wi-Fi provides an FTP server, the app treats onboard flash and microSD as separate disks, and LittleFS is used: [README.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/README.md:7), [file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md:1).
- no FTP implementation beyond a readme in [lib/FtpServer/readme.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/readme.md:1)
- a microSD-only HTTP file server in [WebServer.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:376)
- that HTTP server is only visibly initialized by the webserver test, not normal firmware startup: [test_webserver.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_webserver.cpp:53)
Suggested change: decide the real transfer direction now.
- If HTTP/web UI is the intended path, update README and filesystem docs from “FTP + LittleFS + two disks” to “microSD file access over HTTP/web UI” and delete or mark FTP notes as obsolete.
- If FTP and onboard flash still matter, they are implementation gaps, not documentation details.
2. **Several documented Wi-Fi and UI workflows are still scaffolding.**

The docs describe AP mode, station selection, NTP sync, cloud upload selection/progress, file viewing, and RTC setting: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:3), [wifi-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/wifi-workflow.md:9).
- selecting a configured station, AP, cloud upload, and NTP action only logs: [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:530)
That would avoid the docs reading like finished product behavior while these surfaces are still stubs.
3. **The call-recording UI behavior has drifted from the written UI contract.**

The docs say the user can change call volume, mute/unmute the mic, and see a 3-pixel tally border that becomes dotted when local mic is muted: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:7). The Bluetooth workflow also says the recording screen shows mute status and output volume: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:19).
- exposes push-to-talk mode switching and **speaker** mute in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:185)

- uses a fixed 10-pixel solid red frame in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:21) and [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:403)
- has mic mute support in `AudioManager`, but I did not find GUI wiring for it: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1307)
- has volume APIs, but not a recording-view volume control: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1231)
Suggested change: choose one UI model.
- If the device is primarily push-to-talk on internal hardware, rewrite the docs around P2T and speaker mute.
- If mic mute and volume are still required call controls, add those controls and make the tally border encode mic state as documented.
4. **Recorder performance/file-format docs are slightly ahead of current behavior.**

The file-format doc says new recordings should pre-allocate roughly 1 GB and truncate afterward: [recording-file-format.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/recording-file-format.md:7). The dual-core note also assumes pre-grown `.rec` files: [dual-core-load-distribution.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/dual-core-load-distribution.md:113).

Current code has preallocation implemented but disabled by default: [conf.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:14), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:418).

Also, the doc says packets have a header and payload; the current on-disk implementation writes a **fixed-size** `file_packet_t` for normal `.rec` packets, including a fixed payload array and `payload_length`: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:31), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:230).
- update the format doc to explicitly say `.rec` packets are fixed-size today
- say preallocation is optional/compile-time unless you intend to enable it again
5. **The high-level audio/hardware docs overstate two areas.**

The high-level design says an external WM8960 or SGTL5000 codec may be enabled by build flag: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:9). In the current audio manager, external I2S codec enable functions are explicit “not implemented yet” stubs: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:626).

The same doc says CVSD local PCM is 8 kHz: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:31). The implementation accepts 8 kHz HFP, but `AudioFifo` upsamples 8 kHz input into its 16 kHz internal representation, and recorder packets are labeled `*_16KHZ_MONO`: [AudioFifo.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:90), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:315).
- state “external codec path planned, internal M5Stack audio path implemented”
- HFP CVSD ingress/egress can be 8 kHz
- recorder/internal FIFO normalizes to 16 kHz today

- Bluetooth connection auto-starts a call recording after entering a connected/audio-available state, matching the Bluetooth workflow: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:13), [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:166).

- The recording stop path can also disconnect Bluetooth: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:17), [RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp:64).

- Wi-Fi activation shuts down Bluetooth/audio/recording first, which matches the high-level design: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:17), [WifiManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:291).

- RTC-based recording naming and `.rec` output are in place: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:21), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:68).
- Top bar date/time and battery status are implemented: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:16), [FlyGui.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:630).

Ignoring the eventual removal of `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` as requested: the recording format already has security hooks, but they are placeholders right now. `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` adds nonce fields and leaves encryption/authentication as TODOs: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:39), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:206).
Before hardening transfer/config storage, I would write one short security design doc that states:
- how keys are provisioned and recovered
- what local UI actions are allowed without trust/authentication
- whether the HTTP file surface survives at all
I did not count `bluetooth.json` or `wifi.json` mismatches in the findings. I also did not run builds or tests for this pass; this was a documentation-to-code review.

user_prompt: Implement `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileDecoder.cpp` and its associated header file. It should be a class object
The constructor dictates what the input file name is, and registers callbacks
The class itself has public functions for
- the destructor must clean up correctly, there will be FIFOs involved, larger than what the stack allows
The start function will spin up a FreeRTOS thread, the thread is private, give it a stack size of 2048
the on-complete callback must be fired from poll, not the new thread
all actual file and file system operations must be contained within the new thread
the decoding strategy is a bit simpler than the python script

instantiate two AudioFIFO instances, each with 2048 capacity. One for left channel and one for right channel. The rule is: `file_packet_t` has a field `src`. If `src` is an odd number, the data goes into the left channel (this should be mic), if `src` is an even number, the data goes into the right channel (this should be BT).
The FIFO’s watermark feature should be used, both set to 512 samples (1024 bytes).

open the output file as “temp.wav”, write a fake WAV file RIFF header inside, once we are finished, this will be overwritten. make sure the file write seek pointer is now at the start of stereo 16 bit PCM data.
If both FIFOs can accept at least 512 more samples, then decode a packet, and enqueue it into the correct FIFO. (reminder that the maximum samples per packet is 256, although a size of 240 is likely)

Then, remembering that the watermark feature is used, we have three possible conditions: if only the left FIFO allows for dequeuing, if only the right FIFO allows for dequeuing, or if both FIFOs allow for dequeuing.
If both can be dequeued, then do the dequeue 128 samples from each at a time, interleaved into stereo PCM data and written to the output file.
If only one FIFO can be dequeued, then dequeue 128 samples and create stereo PCM data with the other channel silent, write it to the output file
If the file has been completely read to end, then set the watermark to 0 and continue dequeuing until both FIFOs are empty.
Then the loop is finished. Write the RIFF header correctly. Close the file “temp.wav” and then rename it to the proper name.
Set a flag saying it is complete. Break out of the loop. Delete the thread.

Progress report call back is called every 500ms, but also, only check if that time has elapsed every 8 packet being decoded. Progress is reported by the input file seek pointer vs the input file size.
A cancel flag means the temporary file is deleted and then the loop exits, the thread then ends correctly
Implement decryption as needed according to security level

user_prompt: to `AudioFifo` add a `queueSilence` call that uses `memset` or something like `memset` to add silence to the FIFO, the caller specifies number of samples

agent_reply: Implemented `AudioFifo::queueSilence(size_t sampleCount)` in [AudioFifo.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:95). I checked first and did not find an existing equivalent.

user_prompt: we are making a big change
for unencrypted audio recordings, instead of recording to a custom format file with a `*.rec` extension, instead, we will record to a plain `*.wav` file extension.
we will do some things to make it easy to assemble a stereo 16 bit PCM file in real time even though the mic is optionally on or off and the bluetooth data might be unreliable as well.
first, I've already asked you to add a `queueSilence` to the `AudioFifo`. This is useful for the next change
when writing to the bt2file FIFO, if we know that the mic is off, then we insert silence into mic2file automatically.
when writing to the mic2file FIFO, and we see the bt2file FIFO is significantly behind, then we insert some silence to let it catch up.

when writing to the file, we only write if both FIFO has data so that the data can be mixed into stereo. Since the enqueuing mechanism now attempts to keep both FIFOs at the same level, this should work.
When opening the file, since it is a `*.wav` file in this case, we put a placeholder for the RIFF header. When the file is about to be closed, we put in the correct data into the RIFF header
I've made changes myself, make sure I didn't make mistakes. I've placed comments around the code too, to indicate my intentions. Some documentation have been edited but I might've missed some.
Basically, if `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` is 0, it records to `*.wav` files directly.

Also please make sure that, inside `scripts\thefly_desktop.py`, if the script sees a `*.wav` file instead of a `*.rec` file, it is saved directly to the folder meant for `*.wav` files, skipping the decoding, and if the user asked for it, do the transcription and summarization.
warn me if something is seriously wrong
agent_reply: Implemented the direct `.wav` recording path for `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL == 0`.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:40) now writes `.wav` files for sec0, including placeholder RIFF headers on open and final RIFF headers/truncation on close.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:790) now pumps both file FIFOs into stereo PCM: mic left, BT right, with forced stop-drain using silence for a missing side.

- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:108) now balances the sec0 file FIFOs with silence: exact match when mic is off, catch-up padding when one side falls behind.

- [thefly_desktop.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_desktop.py:180) now saves `.wav` files directly into the `wav` folder and skips decoding; downloaded `.wav` files participate in `--transcribe`.
- Updated the recorder/security docs to reflect `.wav` for unencrypted builds and `.rec` for encrypted builds.

I did not find anything seriously wrong after the pass. The one thing still worth watching on device is real timing behavior during long one-sided streams, since that depends on callback cadence and SD write latency, not just compile-time correctness.
